title: Is AI-based trading actually legit or is my friend just getting lucky?
author: Plastic-Cake-8546
selftext:
Hey everyone, 

I’ve been feeling a bit conflicted lately and wanted to get some perspective.

A close friend of mine has started using this AI trading bot that supposedly uses LLMs to scan the market, do research, and then give him buy/sell suggestions. The crazy part is, he’s actually making decent money with it right now.

On one hand, seeing his profits makes me want to jump in too. But on the other hand, I’m genuinely terrified of the risk. Is AI really at a point where it can consistently "outsmart" the market, or is this just a classic case of a lucky streak in a bull market?

I can’t figure out if LLMs are powerful enough to handle the complexity of trading or if it's all just hype that will eventually crash. Has anyone here had experience with these kinds of bots? Should I give it a shot, or is it better to stay away?

comments.author: sagesofthe4th_world
comments.body:
He may just be showing you his positive PnL. A few wins does not mean it is consistently doing this. Market regime changes frequently.

LLMs need you to feed them some kind of strategy for trading. They have no idea how to trade on their own.

replies.author: Crazy-Pizza8119
replies.body: You build strategy using AI?

replies.author: Busy-Specialist7708
replies.body: I did, I build a killer trading system for FX with AI assistance. But like sage said, I wouldn't let it trade or decide things on its own. Instead I build it as a quant with strict hierarchy, framework and protocols to follow. There is the real power

replies.author: Crazy-Pizza8119
replies.body: Awesome!

replies.author: Crazy-Pizza8119
replies.body: You must be skilled in maths and quant?

replies.author: Busy-Specialist7708
replies.body: No not really. I get dizzy when I see math formulas. It's more macro orientated with all kinds of filters. The AI does the math for me and poops out very useful reports

replies.author: aqua_tango
replies.body: Can you share how you did this?  Inquiring minds want to know.

replies.author: Busy-Specialist7708
replies.body: Well, I spend many hours and days of my free time building this, so don't expect me to hand it out for free. Currently forward testing with an actually small prop firm challenge, I have faith yes. Meanwhile Im still patching, but these get smaller and smaller, so the end is in sight. Once finished I'm gonna run a massive manual backtest. When all is done, then I might throw it out there as a solid product.

replies.author: PurpleImagination263
replies.body: I think me and you are building the same thing lol… my system currently has 120k lines of code and still building!

replies.author: Beautiful-Wasabi-742
replies.body: Still got a long ways to go! Wait until you have a database with millions of lines !

replies.author: Busy-Specialist7708
replies.body: Hmm, depends, cause I'm not really coding anything. Spend most times writing macro economic modules and protocols for AI to operate in. I did vibecoded an app that scans for geo political nonsense, central banks stuff and every friday it reads the CFTC report for me, see if things are crowded or not.

replies.author: Open-Astronomer-5903
replies.body: Funny how we have the same ideas. See my comment ⬆️. The key is that it is able to ingest a large set of data (news) and analyse it to give you a sentiment score. We couldn't, as humans, analyse so much data on our own (or only on 1-2 stocks). I do it on a set of 30 stocks which I'm interested in, + it tries to find out about interesting IPOs.

replies.author: MistaSkizzle
replies.body:
I have been working on something similar. Slowly digesting even just learning trading. I will probably get into Swing trading first and my dashboards that I have been building reflect that data set. 

Out of curiosity, can you share how you've built your dashboard? Which AI you use, what data sets are included, etc? This is what I have been most invested in is the building portion. It is Very cool. I am just getting into the AI myself.

replies.author: sagesofthe4th_world
replies.body:
AI helped most when I was back testing my bot's trading strategy. It helped me to make observations I might not have noticed myself.

My trading bot scripts don't really use AI other than to automate a few rules. 



I mainly used AI to build the scripts and to back test my strategies.

I use very little AI in my actual trading scripts. I probably don't even need to use it. Just  use it to automate some of my script rules. But it's not really necessary.

Big take away is that AI has no idea how to trade on its own. You must give it a strategy. And guardrails.

replies.author: Plastic-Cake-8546
replies.body: It means AI trading will give u a good insight to make  real money 🤑.

replies.author: OkWedding719
replies.body:
The regime point is the key one. Most AI trading systems that show great backtests fail in live markets not because the AI is wrong, but because the regime it trained on no longer exists. A bull market momentum strategy looks like genius until the regime shifts — then the same signals produce the opposite outcome.

LLMs are useful for processing information and generating hypotheses, but they have no inherent understanding of market regime. That context still has to come from somewhere else — whether that's a human reading the tape or a systematic regime filter. Without it, you're just pattern-matching on historical data that may no longer be relevant.

comments.author: Fine-Perspective-438
comments.body:
AI can absolutely help with trading. But not the way most people think. It won't "outsmart" the market on its own. What it does well is process information faster than you can. Treasury announcements, Fed signals, geopolitical risks, earnings data. All at once.

The real edge isn't the AI making trades for you. It's the AI helping you analyze faster and make better decisions. Your friend is probably doing well because he's using AI as a research tool, not a magic button.

If the tool is free and has no deposit or withdrawal access, there's no harm in testing it. I also build something similar called SandClaw, so I'd be curious to hear how they compare.

replies.author: Sophrosyneinc
replies.body: Do you have the link of your trading app called Sandclaw? Would love to check it out, feel free to DM me🙏🏽

[https://github.com/kokogo100/sandclaw](https://github.com/kokogo100/sandclaw)

It is completely free.

replies.body: Lol written by ChatGPT

comments.author: ReceptionUnlucky9455
comments.body: I built an auto trader and called it prop trader pro, it trades every account every day for me. It trades my evaluation and funded accounts from 4 different prop firms with different rules. Some has consistency rule in evaluation accounts and some in funded. Even event trading is one of the conditions. I am also trying self funded accounts from $10k, with prop firm type of strict rules (that helps traders not firms). Let’s see how it goes. I have also built an unambiguous real time signal generator using EMA and bars on the chart. I have tried in 1,2,3,4m timeframes. Built a simple feedback mechanism so that next day trades have some context from previous days. From my honest experience, this program trades much better than my Einstein Buffet in my head. If anyone interested to test, let me know I will send the zip file. It’s wrapped around ninjatrader indicator, so you can load on a chart and continue with your manual efforts on a different chart. Indicator won’t interfere with your usual activities.

replies.author: Plastic-Cake-8546
replies.body: Provide link brother

replies.author: ReceptionUnlucky9455
replies.body: https://technests.ai/products/prop-trader-pro

comments.author: AdEducational4954
comments.body: Seems like all these posts are created so same person can plug what they are selling in the comments under different account.

comments.author: Helpful-Cut-4903
comments.body:
this kind of situation happens a lot honestly. you see someone making money and it’s hard not to feel like you’re missing out. but most of these “ai bots” look great until market conditions change

AI is useful but more as a filter than something that trades for you. i’ve been using finnext: ai stock forecasts for ideas + context and then deciding myself. I wouldn’t trust anything fully automated with real money

comments.author: QuirkyChipmunk1414
comments.body:
Most AI trading tools today are basically:
	
•	pattern recognition
	
•	sentiment + data filtering
	
•	execution support

Not magic.

Your friend might have:
	
•	good timing (bull phase)
	
•	decent risk management
	
•	or just a short hot streak

The real question is:
can it survive a drawdown?

If you try it, treat it like a tool, not a system
small size, track performance, don’t trust blindly

That’s where something like alphamind ai fits better
more decision support than “auto-profit bot”

comments.author: Suspicious-Slip-7808
comments.body:
Creo que la mayoría subestima lo importante que es la estructura.

Un “sistema de trading con IA” no es solo un modelo. Normalmente tenés:

- un scanner
- una capa de decisión
- una capa de ejecución
- y lo más importante: un feedback loop

Lo que casi nadie trackea bien es el “por qué” de cada decisión vs el resultado real. Por ejemplo: por qué entró o rechazó una operación y qué terminó pasando después.

Sin eso no podés mejorar nada. Solo estás mirando resultados sin contexto.

En mi experiencia, el edge no viene de la IA, viene de ese loop.

comments.author: Puzzleheaded_Ad_4478
comments.body:
Yea AI can help make some profit from the market but I won't say it outsmarts the market. It's just a tool giving an edge. 

I've also seen several friends make decent income with the help of AI, and algorithms. It should just be very well crafted (good risky management, trading strategy, position sizing) and be fed by good data.

comments.author: dopple-copter
comments.body: Check back in a year and see if he beats buy/hold

comments.author: LetUsSpeakFreely
comments.body: Depends on what's in the model.  LLMs are nothing more than a bunch of statistical models lumped together. With enough data it could see trends accurately.

comments.author: DAlphaOne
comments.body:
Every time I e asked my clawdbot about a chart he gets it wrong, I have no idea how people accomplish this 😂 I at least get half assed trade reviews.

My buddy built a bot that learns week over week but not sure how it's implemented. 

I just don't know how real it is to try and do active pattern recognition on a chart, it feels too slow. It would be processing significant amounts of data I presume.

comments.author: jabberw0ckee
comments.body:
Check out nof1 the Alpha Arena to see how poorly AI trade.

Most lose significant money. Some got lucky. 

https://share.google/aimode/7tzNCZT7MUBVJdQCX

The best method is to combine algo systems with AI advisors. The algo makes trade signals, the AI analyzes the data and you begin to see which types of conditions, stocks and regimes produce the best outcomes. Then start using the AI to pick the best of the algo signals and trigger a Buy alert. 

Good algos can consistently earn money in almost all but the worst regimes and create loss and drawdown. Most algorithmic solutions to those worst bearish regimes also kill profits while reducing loss. 

The AI can crunch data the Algo produces and quantify trade outcomes to guide future trading and avoid low probability trades and favor higher probability ones.

replies.author: Alert_Attention_5905
replies.body:
I started building exactly this a few months ago, but I scrapped the project because I thought it was a bad idea.

I made a ChatGPT agent use RAG against algorithmic trading books to design an algo trader and then built it, then started building the AI that would manage the algo trader.

But while working on the AI I started thinking my idea was terrible and I just tossed the whole project in an ssd that's now in my closet. Maybe I should load it back up and keep working on it.

replies.author: jabberw0ckee
replies.body:
Yes, reload it. Develop an algo that works well by itself. Even if it just matched alpha or a little better. 

You can backtest and try to use algo methods to mitigate bearish regimes. I tested over 100 scenarios on mine across up to 8 years and mostly 2019 to present, but it made things, net, worse. 

Then try support and resistance detection and chart formation detection and instruct algo what to do. You can use Claude to  create the look back over 3-6 months and what to look for and logic to pass or trade. 

I think you’ll like the results.

comments.author: Key-Bottle7634
comments.body: Pure luck

comments.author: ParaCortex_Bot
comments.body: LLM's can analyse stocks for sure.

comments.author: Emergency-Quiet3210
comments.body: If you are seriously considering LLMs to trade you should probably just stick to buy and hold SPY. LLMs are inherently stochastic, which means there is an element of randomness and thus the system is not repeatable.

comments.author: ReceptionUnlucky9455
comments.body: I use AI to validate my idea, test inputs and outputs and manage edge case scenarios. I forward tested my strategies for past 2+ years. So far taken around 79+ payouts and 15+ rejections. Don’t trade on accounts that don’t permit automation. Tricking some random company is not a long term solution. My idea- do not marry or be impressed with one prop firm. New firms offer greater value sometimes.

There is however a 30% profit share but it does everything so you can't really complain. Send me a DM and we can chat if you are serious about making money. There is of course a risk as well, should go without saying. No 20% monthly profits come without risk.

comments.author: Wizardwizzle
comments.body: Ai is smart, very smart but nothing can outsmart the market. Its not about being smart to make money in the market the results of market movement and price is driven by many random and non random actions so there cant really be a pattern creating a certain price movement

comments.author: StevenVinyl
comments.body:
Works pretty well for me, I'm using a platform which does exactly this, but on top of a trading engine as well. 

  
So I don't even have to prompt the LLM to trade, it "listens" to the market for triggers (mostly using Andean and HMA cloud on specific timeframes) and runs whenever the triggers are hit (usually bullish/bearish crossovers or states).

After that the LLM (usually sonnet 4.6 or qwen 3.5 plus for me) pulls live market data -> analyzes -> decides whether to trade or not (most of the time it doesn't to protect me from bad trades). 

I just come in and check my notifications to see what happened with my runs and if I have positions or not. 

Pretty fun.

replies.author: emlanis
replies.body: You’re doing well.

comments.body: It is legit I use it day in and day out

comments.author: emlanis
comments.body: First and foremost, we’re not in a bull market. It also defends on how well you train and configure the ai trading bot.

comments.author: Important_Voice_4699
comments.body:
I used Gemini and Chatgpt to analyse a few stocks and made about 10% profit in a week. 

Both LLM's seem to have different opinions and strategies but somehow it worked for me. I think it's still too early for me to report that it's a success but I feel AI indeed does have the capabilities to do quick research and suggest.

comments.author: Forsaken_Leader_8
comments.body: It’s usually a bit of both. We’re in a market right now where almost any AI-integrated strategy looks like a work of genius, but the 'luck' runs out the moment the macro trend shifts. The big difference is whether your friend is using a black-box bot that trades for him, or an analytical tool that just gives him an edge. I’ve personally stayed away from fully automated bots because of the 'hallucination' risk, but I’ve been using signalwhisper for the research side lately. It uses LLMs to scan sentiment and news faster than I ever could, but I still make the final call on the trade. It’s basically like having a junior analyst working for you 24/7 without the massive salary. Tell your friend to enjoy the green days but check if his bot has a hard stop-loss for when the AI eventually gets it wrong

comments.author: Sea_Safe2559
comments.body: I have a company that does all this and automates the trading process

replies.body: Link ? Website ?

comments.body: Yes AI helps, especially with backtesting

comments.author: WaterYouTalmbout
comments.body: Make sure to have a historical database of candlesticks so you can run replay tests on all ya strategies!

comments.author: Open-Astronomer-5903
comments.body:
I spent a long time polishing a trading bot with a lot of factors. Designed for day-trading, managing portfolio, and launched several times per day. 
Giving a score to companies correlated by the sector sentiment depending on all the news it can find, associated with a score depending on several standard maths instruments (golden scross on moving averages, overbought / oversold factor, institutional volumes, etc). Depending on these scores, it gives instructions. Buy this immediately, or buy because you have cash available, sell this to buy that, sell IMMEDIATELY because it's about to collapse, etc. Also tells you "put a stop loss at that value, or trailing stop with these params, etc).
I added instructions on trading style. Win small but often, cut your losses to lose small when you lose, win more often than you lose, etc.
Also, it has a built-in kill switch when the market is too volatile (basically, it's chaos, can't predict anything, stay put).
Added a gamification factor. It gives point to itself when he does good and bad points when it loses, with tiers depending on perf, plus instructions to become better. Apprentice, confirmed trader, expert, etc.

I'm in testing phase, but so far, even though it loses sometimes, it consistently wins more often, in the order of 1-2% per day. I'll keep the simulation longer before putting more money, but so far, it's pretty encouraging.

Using Opus, which is impressive in interpreting news and giving me full analysis. It picked up some news about companies being awarded big contracts and buy asap, which gave 3% on a single stock within 2 hours.

After it won 2-3%, it consistently tells you to sell to take your gains instead of risking, unless if strong positive signals, mitigated by trailing stop orders.

I'll see how it develops, but so far, I'm very impressed by how well it works. (Made a full agent framework with skills, specialised subagents, specialised coordinator agent, code to calculate all instruments, etc) 

1 thing to note is that I had to make it create a scrapper of ticker websites (apis  are paid) instead of Yahoo Finance, which is 1h behind).

comments.author: ZealousHarp2237
comments.body: AI can help analyze data faster, but consistent profits still depend on strategy, risk management, and market conditions. Luck can play a big role early on, so it’s best to stay cautious and treat it as a tool, not a guarantee

comments.author: Traditional_Ear5237
comments.body: which one are you using? yeagers or coincindence?

comments.author: Zayrox_ai
comments.body:
AI trading is legit but not in the way most people think.
Your friend might be making money right now, but that doesn’t automatically mean the AI is “beating the market.” In many cases, it’s a mix of good timing, market conditions (like a bull run), and basic strategies working well temporarily.
LLMs and AI tools are powerful for analysis, scanning data, and finding patterns—but they don’t predict the market with certainty. Markets are influenced by too many unpredictable factors.
The real edge isn’t just “using AI”it’s: 
 Having a solid strategy
 Managing risk properly
 Staying consistent
AI can help remove emotions and improve decisions, but it’s not a magic money machine.
If you’re thinking of trying it: Start small, test on demo or low capital, and focus on learning how it works rather than chasing profits
